en.subheading: architect of the gothic and modern mystery
en.heroDescription: Poe's poems and tales fuse melodic, atmospheric language with psychological intensity; from The Raven to The Murder in the Rue Morgue he explored grief, madness, and detection while pioneering the “unity of effect” that still shapes literature today.
en.introText:
Edgar Allan Poe was a poet, writer and literary critic from Massachusetts (USA) who wrote many influential pieces of literature. Edgar had a gothic and dramatic style that has made him a well known poet even more than 100 years later. He covered themes of mystery, loss, grief, murder, and the darker side of the human psyche.

Edgar Allan Poe is often strongly associated with the gothic and macabre poems, with pieces such as The Raven and The Conqueror Worm, but he contributed to more than that. Edgar was a strongly passionate writer and critic, focusing on both the thematic and technical aspects of writing with his “Unity of Effect”, where all parts of a short text should contribute to a single idea, thought or feeling. Furthermore, Edgar is often considered to be the inventor of the detective and murder mystery genres with his 1841 story The Murder in the Rue Morgue.

Despite his many strong works and love of literature, Edgar experienced many tragedies throughout his life. Abandoned as a child, adopted by a father that was constantly disappointed with him, struggling financially, and losing many to tuberculosis. It is almost certain that these experiences influenced Edgar both as a person and a writer. Many of Edgar's pieces cover themes of loss, death, grief and despair that he himself must have had a fair share of. The most famous of these pieces being The Raven.
en.infoText:
Edgar [Allan] Poe was born in Boston (Massachusetts, USA) on the 19 of January 1809. Edgar Poe was born to David and Elizabeth Poe, brother to 2 year old Willian Henry Leonard Poe (Henry Poe), Edgar's life was a rocky one from the beginning.

David, father to Edgar and Henry, abandoned the family in 1810, one year after Edgar was born. This betrayal was quickly turned into catastrophe when Elizabeth, Edgar's mother, died only one year later from tuberculosis. This left Edgar and Henry as young orphans.

Edgar and Henry were quickly taken in, but by different families. Henry went to his paternal grandparents, the parents of the father that abandoned him and his brother. While Edgar was taken in by John and Frances Allan, though never formally adopted. This is where Edgar Poe becomes Edgar Allan Poe, taking on the last name of his unofficial foster parents

In his early teenage years, Edgar demonstrated a great deal of interest in literature. At the age of 18, Edgar published his first book named Tamerlane and Other Poems. The book contained many different poems, showcasing Edgar's signature love of poetry.

During his childhood and early adulthood, Edgar's relationship with his father had constant strain. This resulted in a falling out in 1827, after Edgar experienced high financial problems due to his gambling habits. This was only a short time before Edgar initiated his literary career with Tamerlane and Other Poems

After leaving the University of Virginia, where he studied for only one year, due to financial troubles, Edgar had a variety of occupations. He spent some time in the army, participated in writing competitions, and worked as an editor and literary critic.

In 1845 Edgar wrote his most famous work: The Raven. The Raven was a gothic and melodic description of a man's encounter with a strange raven. The man narrates the poem as he slowly is driven to madness. The poem has many interpretations, but most agree that it greatly embodies themes of grief and loss

Only four years after releasing The Raven, Edgar Allen Poe was found in a delirious state in Baltimore wearing clothes that were not his own. A few days after being found, Edgar Allan Poe dies mysteriously, leaving little to no information regarding his cause of death or the events leading up to it

The Raven remains as one of the most famous poems ever written. It carries with it the mystery of who Edgar Allan Poe was as a person, as well as the miseries and tragedies he had experienced in his life. Edgar may be gone, but there is a lot The Raven can teach us about Edgar's views on loss, grief, and writing
en.englishRaven:
Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.
“'Tis some visitor,” I muttered, “tapping at my chamber door—
Only this and nothing more.”

Ah, distinctly I remember it was in the bleak December;
And each separate dying ember wrought its ghost upon the floor.
Eagerly I wished the morrow;—vainly I had sought to borrow
From my books surcease of sorrow—sorrow for the lost Lenore—
For the rare and radiant maiden whom the angels name Lenore—
Nameless here for evermore.

And the silken, sad, uncertain rustling of each purple curtain
Thrilled me—filled me with fantastic terrors never felt before;
So that now, to still the beating of my heart, I stood repeating
“'Tis some visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door—
Some late visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door;—
This it is and nothing more.”

Presently my soul grew stronger; hesitating then no longer,
“Sir,” said I, “or Madam, truly your forgiveness I implore;
But the fact is I was napping, and so gently you came rapping,
And so faintly you came tapping, tapping at my chamber door,
That I scarce was sure I heard you”—here I opened wide the door;—
Darkness there and nothing more.

Deep into that darkness peering, long I stood there wondering, fearing,
Doubting, dreaming dreams no mortal ever dared to dream before;
But the silence was unbroken, and the stillness gave no token,
And the only word there spoken was the whispered word, “Lenore?”
This I whispered, and an echo murmured back the word, “Lenore!”—
Merely this and nothing more.

Back into the chamber turning, all my soul within me burning,
Soon again I heard a tapping somewhat louder than before.
“Surely,” said I, “surely that is something at my window lattice;
Let me see, then, what thereat is, and this mystery explore—
Let my heart be still a moment and this mystery explore;—
'Tis the wind and nothing more!”

Open here I flung the shutter, when, with many a flirt and flutter,
In there stepped a stately Raven of the saintly days of yore;
Not the least obeisance made he; not a minute stopped or stayed he;
But, with mien of lord or lady, perched above my chamber door—
Perched upon a bust of Pallas just above my chamber door—
Perched, and sat, and nothing more.

Then this ebony bird beguiling my sad fancy into smiling,
By the grave and stern decorum of the countenance it wore,
“Though thy crest be shorn and shaven, thou,” I said, “art sure no craven,
Ghastly grim and ancient Raven wandering from the Nightly shore—
Tell me what thy lordly name is on the Night's Plutonian shore!”
Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

Much I marvelled this ungainly fowl to hear discourse so plainly,
Though its answer little meaning—little relevancy bore;
For we cannot help agreeing that no living human being
Ever yet was blessed with seeing bird above his chamber door—
Bird or beast upon the sculptured bust above his chamber door,
With such name as “Nevermore.”

But the Raven, sitting lonely on the placid bust, spoke only
That one word, as if his soul in that one word he did outpour.
Nothing farther then he uttered—not a feather then he fluttered—
Till I scarcely more than muttered “Other friends have flown before—
On the morrow he will leave me, as my Hopes have flown before.”
Then the bird said “Nevermore.”

Startled at the stillness broken by reply so aptly spoken,
“Doubtless,” said I, “what it utters is its only stock and store
Caught from some unhappy master whom unmerciful Disaster
Followed fast and followed faster till his songs one burden bore—
Till the dirges of his Hope that melancholy burden bore
Of 'Never—nevermore'.”

But the Raven still beguiling all my fancy into smiling,
Straight I wheeled a cushioned seat in front of bird, and bust and door;
Then, upon the velvet sinking, I betook myself to linking
Fancy unto fancy, thinking what this ominous bird of yore—
What this grim, ungainly, ghastly, gaunt, and ominous bird of yore
Meant in croaking “Nevermore.”

This I sat engaged in guessing, but no syllable expressing
To the fowl whose fiery eyes now burned into my bosom's core;
This and more I sat divining, with my head at ease reclining
On the cushion's velvet lining that the lamp-light gloated o'er,
But whose velvet-violet lining with the lamp-light gloating o'er,
She shall press, ah, nevermore!

Then, methought, the air grew denser, perfumed from an unseen censer
Swung by Seraphim whose foot-falls tinkled on the tufted floor.
“Wretch,” I cried, “thy God hath lent thee—by these angels he hath sent thee
Respite—respite and nepenthe from thy memories of Lenore;
Quaff, oh quaff this kind nepenthe and forget this lost Lenore!”
Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

“Prophet!” said I, “thing of evil!—prophet still, if bird or devil!—
Whether Tempter sent, or whether tempest tossed thee here ashore,
Desolate yet all undaunted, on this desert land enchanted—
On this home by Horror haunted—tell me truly, I implore—
Is there—is there balm in Gilead?—tell me—tell me, I implore!”
Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

“Prophet!” said I, “thing of evil!—prophet still, if bird or devil!
By that Heaven that bends above us—by that God we both adore—
Tell this soul with sorrow laden if, within the distant Aidenn,
It shall clasp a sainted maiden whom the angels name Lenore—
Clasp a rare and radiant maiden whom the angels name Lenore.”
Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

“Be that word our sign of parting, bird or fiend!” I shrieked, upstarting—
“Get thee back into the tempest and the Night's Plutonian shore!
Leave no black plume as a token of that lie thy soul hath spoken!
Leave my loneliness unbroken!—quit the bust above my door!
Take thy beak from out my heart, and take thy form from off my door!”
Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

And the Raven, never flitting, still is sitting, still is sitting
On the pallid bust of Pallas just above my chamber door;
And his eyes have all the seeming of a demon's that is dreaming,
And the lamp-light o'er him streaming throws his shadow on the floor;
And my soul from out that shadow that lies floating on the floor
Shall be lifted—nevermore!
en.viewText:
Edgar Allan Poe had a profound influence on literature, particularly in detective fiction, horror, and psychological storytelling. His character C. Auguste Dupin laid the groundwork for modern detective stories and inspired writers like Arthur Conan Doyle. In horror, Poe shifted focus from external monsters to the fears and obsessions of the human mind, exploring themes of madness, guilt, and grief, which later influenced authors such as H. P. Lovecraft.

In his poem The Raven, Poe examines grief and mental deterioration. The repeated word “Nevermore” symbolizes the narrator's despair and obsessive thoughts. Critical views vary: some once considered his work overly dark, but today he is celebrated for his innovation, emotional depth, and lasting impact.

Personally, we think Edgar was one of the greatest tortured writers of this millennium. His melodic and moody prose paint pictures of scenes unlike any other author ever has. We think his influence has been a positive expansion of the emotional and psychological situations possible to depict in text.
en.analyText:
The poem begins by establishing that it takes place on a cold night in December with the narrator, reading a book in his study, falling asleep. This establishes the gothic theme and setting of the poem and marks the narrator as a scholar, the mention of the narrator falling asleep additionally makes it unclear whether or not the events of the story are real or if they may be just a dream.

Suddenly the narrator is awoken by a knocking on his door, he assures himself that it is just a visitor and nothing more, this shows that the narrator is a rational person but the fact he had to assure himself at all indicates his uncertainty, showcasing both his doubt and rationale.

The narrator then explains that he was reading in a failed attempt to distract himself from Lenore, his dead lover, this establishes that the narrator is grief-stricken by the death of Lenore, but also shows that the memory of his lover is a curse he wishes he could escape.

The narrator is then frightened by the curtains rustling, and he yet again tells himself that it is nothing more than a visitor, yet again attempting to remove his fear that it may be supernatural, all this effort to assure himself that the knock is entirely rational shows that his rationale is already under siege from his grief over Lenore. Finding some amount of courage, he calls out to the person knocking at the door, apologising for taking so long because he was napping.

After opening the door the narrator finds that there is nobody there, he whispers into the darkness “Lenore” but hears only his echo and nothing more, this shows that though the narrator started the poem trying to forget Lenore, he still holds an irrational hope that she could return showing that while he may be fearful, he wants the supernatural to occur so that he may see her again.

Then, the narrator hears a knocking on his window, and he opens it, a raven flying in and landing on a bust of Pallas. Pallas is the Greco-Roman goddess of wisdom and learning, the bird sitting on it showing an opposition to such rationality. The narrator, amused by the raven's visage, asks it for its name, the bird responds “nevermore.” The narrator remarks to himself that the raven must have learnt it from a previous owner, he then grabs a chair and places it in front of the raven, trying to understand what it means by “nevermore.” The narrator showcases his rationale yet again by quickly developing an answer as to how the raven learned this word, but his curiosity drives him to want to understand the bird in spite of it likely having no meaning.

The narrator senses that the air has grown denser, indicating the presence of angels sent by God to help him recover from his grief, he wonders if he could consume the nepenthe, a mythological potion of forgetfulness, to rid him of his memories of Lenore, the raven answering “nevermore.” The narrator, now throwing out his rationale and taking the raven seriously, is crushed by the raven's answer.

The narrator, growing more anxious, asks the raven if there is any hope of reuniting with Lenore, the raven responds “nevermore.” The narrator asks if they may meet again in eden, yet again being answered “nevermore.” The narrator, now furious at the bird, screams at it to leave and never return. The narrator's relentless questioning of the raven in spite of it's singular answer show that the narrator's rationality has not only failed in understanding the bird, but has pushed him to near madness. In his fervor to understand the bird's meaning he has lost sight of the fact that the bird may not hold any meaning, his mind slipping into an interpretive frenzy, looking for a meaning in everything, and viewing the raven's “nevermore” as denying all his hopes of reuniting with Lenore.

As the poem ends, the narrator becomes overcome by despair, the raven still sitting on the bust of Pallas. the narrator concludes by saying he still lives in the bird's inescapable shadow. The raven's refusal to leave parallels the memories of Lenore, suggesting that death and grief are inescapable, additionally the narrator still living in the raven's shadow implies that his ability to reason remains diminished by the unknowable. Ultimately it doesn't matter if the raven is supernatural, the product of the narrator's dream, or a normal bird that learned one word. In each case it is the narrator's own doubts in the face of loss, memory, and the unknown that have driven him to madness.
en.modernText:
Edgar Allan Poe's poem The Raven has many connections to how we feel today, even though it was written a long time ago (1845). The poem is about a man grieving for his lost love, Lenore. When a raven suddenly enters his room and repeats the word "Nevermore", he begins to interpret it as a sign that he will never see her again. The longer the night goes on, the more he loses control of his emotions.

A clear connection to the present is how the poem shows mental illness. The narrator gets stuck in negative thoughts and ruminates all the time. He interprets everything in a dark way and makes the situation worse. It is something that many people can recognize today, for example in anxiety or depression. It is easy to get stuck in a spiral of thoughts where everything feels hopeless.

Another similarity is loneliness. The narrator sits alone in the middle of the night with his thoughts, and this makes the sadness even stronger. Today, many people also feel lonely, even though we have social media and can talk to each other all the time, it doesn't always help.

There are also differences between then and now. For example, in the 19th century, people didn't talk openly about mental illness and there wasn't the same help as there is today. Now there is therapy, school counselors and more knowledge about how to deal with grief and anxiety. At the same time, we live in a more stressful society where pressure and demands can have a much greater impact.

I think The Raven is still relevant because it shows how strongly our thoughts affect us. Grief and loneliness are feelings that will always be there, even though society has changed, human feelings are the same.

sv.subheading: arkitekten bakom gotiken och den moderna detektivberättelsen.
sv.heroDescription: Hans verk förenar melodiskt, suggestivt språk med psykologisk skärpa; från The Raven till The Murder in the Rue Morgue utforskade han sorg, galenskap och detektivik, samtidigt som hans princip om en enhetlig verkan (”unity of effect”) fortfarande formar litterär teknik i dag.
sv.introText:
Edgar Allan Poe var en poet, författare och litteraturkritiker från Massachusetts som skrev många inflytelserika verk. Han hade en gotisk och dramatisk stil som gjorde honom välkänd långt efter sin samtid. Han behandlade teman som mysterier, förlust, sorg, mord och människans mörkare psyke.

Han förknippas ofta med gotiska och makabra dikter, till exempel The Raven och The Conqueror Worm, men gjorde mer än så. Han var en passionerad författare och kritiker som lade lika stor vikt vid tematik som vid teknik i skrivandet, inte minst genom sin idé om ”unity of effect”, där alla delar av en kort text bör bidra till en enda idé, tanke eller känsla. Dessutom räknas han ofta som en av upphovsmännen till detektiv och mordmysterium genrerna tack vare berättelsen The Murders in the Rue Morgue.

Trots sina starka verk och sin kärlek till litteraturen mötte han många tragedier under livet. Han övergavs som barn, växte upp hos en far som ofta var besviken på honom, hade ekonomiska svårigheter och förlorade flera närstående i tuberkulos. Dessa erfarenheter påverkade honom med största sannolikhet både som människa och som författare. Många av hans verk handlar om förlust, död, sorg och förtvivlan, teman han sannolikt själv upplevt i hög grad. Den mest kända av dessa är dock den förstnämnda.
sv.infoText:
Edgar [Allan] Poe föddes i Boston den 19 januari 1809. Han var son till David och Elizabeth Poe och bror till William Henry Leonard Poe (Henry Poe) som då var två år gammal. Från början var Edgars liv stormigt.

Redan 1810 övergav fadern familjen, och året därpå avled modern, vilket lämnade Edgar och Henry som små föräldralösa. De togs om hand av olika släktingar: Henry placerades hos sina farföräldrar, medan Edgar hamnade hos John och Frances Allan, utan att formellt adopteras. Det är här han börjar bära namnet Edgar Allan Poe, “Allan” från sin nya familj.

Som tonåring visade han tidigt ett starkt intresse för litteratur. Vid arton års ålder gav han ut sin första samling, Tamerlane and Other Poems, som innehöll flera dikter och redan visade hans förkärlek för poesin.

Under barndomen och den unga vuxenåldern var relationen till Allan ständigt ansträngd. Bråket kulminerade 1827, delvis på grund av ekonomiska problem och spelvanor, strax innan Poe inledde sin litterära bana med Tamerlane and Other Poems. Efter att ha lämnat University of Virginia efter bara ett år på grund av pengar försörjde han sig på olika sätt: militärtjänst, tävlingar i skrivande, samt arbete som redaktör och litteraturkritiker.

År 1845 publicerade han sin mest kända dikt, The Raven, en gotisk, melodisk skildring av en mans möte med en gåtfull korp, som sakta driver honom till vansinne. Dikten tolkas på många sätt, men ses i allmänhet som ett starkt uttryck för sorg och förlust.

Endast fyra år efter publiceringen hittades Poe i ett delirium i Baltimore, klädd i någon annans kläder. Några dagar senare avled han under oklara omständigheter; omständigheterna kring hans död och dess orsak förblev i stort sett obevarade.

The Raven förblir en av de mest berömda dikterna som någonsin skrivits. Den rymmer gåtan kring vem Edgar Allan Poe var som människa, liksom de vedermödor och tragedier han upplevt i sitt liv. Han må vara borta, men dikten har mycket att säga om hans syn på förlust, sorg och skrivande.
sv.englishRaven:
Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.
“'Tis some visitor,” I muttered, “tapping at my chamber door—
Only this and nothing more.”

Ah, distinctly I remember it was in the bleak December;
And each separate dying ember wrought its ghost upon the floor.
Eagerly I wished the morrow;—vainly I had sought to borrow
From my books surcease of sorrow—sorrow for the lost Lenore—
For the rare and radiant maiden whom the angels name Lenore—
Nameless here for evermore.

And the silken, sad, uncertain rustling of each purple curtain

So that now, to still the beating of my heart, I stood repeating
“'Tis some visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door—
Some late visitor entreating entrance at my chamber door;—
This it is and nothing more.”

Presently my soul grew stronger; hesitating then no longer,
“Sir,” said I, “or Madam, truly your forgiveness I implore;
But the fact is I was napping, and so gently you came rapping,
And so faintly you came tapping, tapping at my chamber door,

Darkness there and nothing more.

Deep into that darkness peering, long I stood there wondering, fearing,

But the silence was unbroken, and the stillness gave no token,
And the only word there spoken was the whispered word, “Lenore?”
This I whispered, and an echo murmured back the word, “Lenore!”—
Merely this and nothing more.

Back into the chamber turning, all my soul within me burning,
Soon again I heard a tapping somewhat louder than before.
“Surely,” said I, “surely that is something at my window lattice;
Let me see, then, what thereat is, and this mystery explore—
Let my heart be still a moment and this mystery explore;—
'Tis the wind and nothing more!”

Open here I flung the shutter, when, with many a flirt and flutter,
In there stepped a stately Raven of the saintly days of yore;
Not the least obeisance made he; not a minute stopped or stayed he;

Perched upon a bust of Pallas just above my chamber door—
Perched, and sat, and nothing more.

Then this ebony bird beguiling my sad fancy into smiling,

“Though thy crest be shorn and shaven, thou,” I said, “art sure no craven,
Ghastly grim and ancient Raven wandering from the Nightly shore—
Tell me what thy lordly name is on the Night's Plutonian shore!”

Much I marvelled this ungainly fowl to hear discourse so plainly,

Bird or beast upon the sculptured bust above his chamber door,

But the Raven still beguiling all my fancy into smiling,

This I sat engaged in guessing, but no syllable expressing

“Prophet!” said I, “thing of evil!—prophet still, if bird or devil!—

“Prophet!” said I, “thing of evil!—prophet still, if bird or devil!
By that Heaven that bends above us—by that God we both adore—

Clasp a rare and radiant maiden whom the angels name Lenore.”

Shall be lifted—nevermore!
sv.swedishRaven:
En gång vid en dyster midnatt, medan jag grubblade, svag och trött,
över många märkliga och underliga volymer av bortglömd lärdom—
medan jag nickade, nästan slumrande, kom plötsligt ett knackande,
som av någon som varsamt knackade, knackade på min kammardörr.
”Det är någon besökare,” mumlade jag, ”som knackar på min kammardörr—
endast detta och inget mer.”

Åh, klart minns jag att det var i den dystra december;
och varje enskild döende glöd kastade sitt spöke på golvet.
Ivrigt önskade jag morgondagen; förgäves hade jag sökt låna
från mina böcker lindring från sorg—sorg över den förlorade Lenore—
den sällsynta och strålande jungfrun som änglarna kallar Lenore—
namnlös här för evigt mer.

Och det silkesmjuka, sorgsna, osäkra prasslandet från varje purpurfärgad gardin
skakade mig—fyllde mig med fantastiska fasor som jag aldrig tidigare känt;
så att jag nu, för att stilla mitt hjärtas bultande, stod och upprepade:
”Det är någon besökare som ber om inträde vid min kammardörr—
någon sen besökare som ber om inträde vid min kammardörr;
det är detta och inget mer.”

Snart blev min själ starkare; och utan att längre tveka
sade jag: ”Herre, eller Fru, sannerligen ber jag om er förlåtelse;
men faktum är att jag höll på att slumra, och så försiktigt kom ni och knackade,
och så svagt kom ni och knackade, knackade på min kammardörr,
att jag knappt var säker på att jag hörde er”—här öppnade jag dörren vid;
mörker där och inget mer.

Djupt in i det mörkret stirrande stod jag länge där, undrande, fruktande,
tvivlande, drömmande drömmar som ingen dödlig någonsin vågat drömma förut;
men tystnaden var obruten, och stillheten gav inget tecken,
och det enda ord som uttalades där var det viskade ordet: ”Lenore?”
detta viskade jag, och ett eko mumlade tillbaka ordet: ”Lenore!”
endast detta och inget mer.

När jag vände tillbaka in i kammaren, hela min själ brinnande inom mig,
hörde jag snart igen ett knackande, något högre än förut.
”Säkert”, sade jag, ”säkert är det något vid mitt fönstergaller;
låt mig se då vad som finns där, och utforska detta mysterium—
låt mitt hjärta vara stilla ett ögonblick och utforska detta mysterium;
det är vinden och inget mer!”

Här kastade jag upp fönsterluckan, när, med många flax och fladdranden,
steg där in en ståtlig korp från de heliga dagarna förr;
inte den minsta hälsning gjorde han; inte en minut stannade eller dröjde han;
utan, med hållning som en herre eller dam, satte han sig ovanför min kammardörr—
satte sig på en byst av Pallas just ovanför min kammardörr—
satte sig, och satt, och inget mer.

Då denna ebenholtsfågel lockade min sorgsna fantasi till ett leende,
genom det allvarliga och stränga uttryck som dess ansikte bar,
”Fast din hjässprydnad är avklippt och rakad, du”, sade jag, ”är sannerligen ingen feg,
spöklik, bister och uråldrig korp vandrande från nattens strand—
säg mig vad ditt ädla namn är på nattens plutoniska strand!”
sade korpen: ”Aldrig mer.”

Jag förundrades mycket över att denna klumpiga fågel talade så tydligt,
även om dess svar bar liten mening—liten relevans;
ty vi kan inte annat än hålla med om att ingen levande människa
någonsin ännu har blivit välsignad med att se en fågel ovanför sin kammardörr—
fågel eller djur på den skulpterade bysten ovanför sin kammardörr,
med ett sådant namn som ”Aldrig mer”.

Men korpen, sittande ensam på den stilla bysten, sade endast
detta enda ord, som om hans själ i det enda ordet utgöts.
Inget mer uttalade han då—inte en fjäder rörde han—
tills jag knappt mer än mumlade: ”Andra vänner har flugit bort förut—
i morgon kommer han lämna mig, liksom mina Hopp har flugit bort förut.”
då sade fågeln: ”Aldrig mer.”

Förskräckt över att tystnaden brutits av ett svar så träffande uttalat,
sade jag: ”Utan tvivel är det han yttrar hela hans lager och förråd,
fångat från någon olycklig herre som obarmhärtig Olycka
följde snabbt och följde snabbare tills hans sånger bar en enda börda—
tills klagosångerna över hans Hopp bar den sorgsna bördan
av 'Aldrig—aldrig mer'.”

Men korpen, fortfarande lockande all min fantasi till leende,
rullade jag genast fram en stoppad stol framför fågeln och bysten och dörren;
och sedan, när jag sjönk ner i sammeten, började jag länka
fantasi till fantasi, tänkande vad denna olycksbådande fågel från förr—
vad denna bistra, klumpiga, spöklika, magra och olycksbådande fågel från förr
menade när den kraxade ”Aldrig mer”.

Detta satt jag sysselsatt med att gissa, men utan att yttra en stavelse
till fågeln vars brinnande ögon nu brände in i mitt hjärtas kärna;
detta och mer satt jag och anade, med mitt huvud bekvämt lutat
mot kudden av sammet som lampans sken vilade på,
men vars sammetsvioletta foder under lampans sken
hon skall trycka, åh, aldrig mer!

Då tyckte jag att luften blev tätare, parfymerad från ett osynligt rökelsekar
svängt av serafer vars fotsteg klingade på det tuftade golvet.
”Eländige”, ropade jag, ”din Gud har lånat dig—genom dessa änglar har han sänt dig
vila—vila och nepenthes från dina minnen av Lenore;
drick, åh drick denna vänliga nepenthes och glöm denna förlorade Lenore!”
sade korpen: ”Aldrig mer.”

”Profet!” sade jag, ”ondskans ting!—profet ändå, vare sig fågel eller djävul!—
vare sig Frestaren sände dig, eller om stormen kastade dig hit i land,
öde men ändå oförskräckt, på detta förtrollade ödeland—
i detta hem hemsökt av Skräck—säg mig sanningen, jag ber dig—
finns det—finns det balsam i Gilead? säg mig—säg mig, jag ber dig!”
sade korpen: ”Aldrig mer.”

”Profet!” sade jag, ”ondskans ting!—profet ändå, vare sig fågel eller djävul!
vid den Himmel som böjer sig över oss—vid den Gud vi båda tillber—
säg denna själ tyngd av sorg om den, i det avlägsna Eden,
ska omfamna en helig jungfru som änglarna kallar Lenore—
omfamna en sällsynt och strålande jungfru som änglarna kallar Lenore.”
sade korpen: ”Aldrig mer.”

”Må det ordet vara vårt tecken på avsked, fågel eller demon!” skrek jag och reste mig—
”ta dig tillbaka in i stormen och till nattens plutoniska strand!
lämna ingen svart fjäder som ett tecken på den lögn din själ har talat!
lämna min ensamhet obruten! lämna bysten ovanför min dörr!
ta din näbb ur mitt hjärta och ta din gestalt från min dörr!”
sade korpen: ”Aldrig mer.”

Och korpen, utan att flyga bort, sitter fortfarande, sitter fortfarande
på den bleka bysten av Pallas just ovanför min kammardörr;
och hans ögon har allt utseende av en demon som drömmer,
och lampans ljus som strömmar över honom kastar hans skugga på golvet;
och min själ från den skugga som ligger flytande på golvet
ska lyftas—aldrig mer.
